Hey, it's Sean and Andre. This is the art of email workshop. Today's format is going to be a series of questions. There's some questions we've been thinking about after reading feedback, getting feedback by email, comments, and just other ideas we've been bouncing around with each other. And the way I've set this up for today is I created a list of questions. I'll ask the question. I'll share a couple of thoughts and I'm going to hand it off to Andre and we're just going to bounce back and forth within these thoughts and see where it leads. We have a long list and we want to get through. I think we'll probably get through all of them. OK, first question really is around the different pathways into an art of email inspired system. So let's talk about, I say pathways, let's explain that a little bit more. So you can imagine one potential way somebody could enter your world. They could opt in, general opt in, go to a relationship building series within then value newsletter. This is very common for a content strategy, also very common for a lead generation strategy. So for us, for example, if you go to our site, there's a subscribe button. It has an overview about who we are, what we believe, who we're for. And at the end of that, you can opt in and you get a relationship building series, which is our seven part emergency marketing series, which is general. And if you were to read any of the content on our site, on the content that we've written and sent out to our newsletter that we have repurposed on our site, you'll see at the top, there's a box that says, hey, you can subscribe. If this is interesting to you, you can subscribe. So we send paid traffic to that. And that's a way to generate leads. That's one pathway that you could use to enter an art of email system. Another pathway, certainly this happens for many people when somebody makes a purchase. You have a course or you have a product or you have something. Someone goes to your site, they find you in Google. If they see your ads, whatever it is, they see the thing that you've created and they purchase it. This could be a front end offer, lower cost front end offer if you're sending paid traffic to it. It could be a higher cost offer that you're sending paid traffic to. You could be getting organic traffic. You could have a YouTube channel where you teach people something and then that points back to your website and they get to your website and they buy. There are a million different ways and that's a purchase that would lead to a customer onboarding series in that example. And a third scenario I'll talk about before I hand it over to Andre to really explore this is you can also have people who are interested in a product not necessarily interested in you. Now, this is fuzzy though, right? So sometimes people, and I'll give the example, it's just for the sake of argument say that you have a YouTube channel where you teach people how to whatever use a certain piece of software really well or get the most from the certain piece of software and then you have a course on that. Well, people are exposed to you and their YouTube channel and they see your videos, but when they come to your site and they're more interested in the product, so your relationship building series is, it has a little bit of a different vibe to it because yes, you're building a relationship to them as an individual, but you're also recognizing that they came interested in this course, for example, that you sell. So it is a relationship building series, but it's one that puts the course in the value you have front and center and may have a soft bridge to a story powered promotion. For example, that would be a typical implementation for that. So Andre, any, I mean, react to anything that I said or just throw in different use cases or I mean, I know there are infinite number of ways someone could use the art of email, what I'm trying to come at. This question is, how can we look at some of those ways they could use it and then direct them toward a pathway into the art of email or at least how to think about it. We can't answer every, we can't say, we can't make a list of a thousand different ways to use it, but I think what we can do is we can give people an idea how to think about how to use the art of email and then they can formulate it to match their needs specifically. Yeah, I think that's one of the things, once you've been through the whole of art of email, that it's very robust in the sense that you get to construct this thing in any way that matches how you want to do business. And I think those three use cases or those three scenarios, each of which has a few nuances or many nuances will typically cover most of the basis. The third one you mentioned, which is a product centric entry point. So somebody lands on a landing page that could be from, so they land on a product landing page. So they land on the art of email landing page, for example. So there's obviously copy there, there might be an ability to make a purchase right there and then, but then there's also an opt-in, which that's somebody's... They just want more, they can see what's on the page, they're not ready to bar, well, they're not ready to bar in this moment. So they add themselves to the email list, which is a product centric one, and as Sean mentioned, they don't necessarily, in that context, want to build a relationship with, insert Guru's name, or Sean and Andre, for example, they might be more interested in the actual email course or whatever the course is. And it also could, those referrals or those people landing on the page could come from an affiliate relationship. An affiliate has mentioned that, or someone's read a content piece on somebody else's website, that's talking about the email product specifically, not necessarily about Andre and Sean, for example. So when they click through, they just want to know about the product. So that RBS, that relationship building series, is going to be far more focused around the product and less about who Andre and Sean are. Obviously within the context of that relationship building series, Andre and Sean are going to demonstrate their authority within that, although that's not going to be the front and center thing, it'll be more an implicit thing, and they'll get to know us behind the product. And then as Sean mentioned, there could be a soft bridge that then goes straight to a story-part promotion, and then to the newsletter. I think that's just going a bit deeper with that scenario. I don't know if you see an example that I shared with YouTube, because I think it's a perfect one. It happened to me and it's something that somebody else can go out and they can replicate so they can see it in real time. So earlier this year, it's 2021, when we are recording this, earlier this year, I stumbled on a YouTube video by someone named Nick Milo. And Nick Milo has created a course called, the video was about a PKM, personal knowledge management software called Obsidian. And Nick had the email that showed up was basically that, this is why Obsidian is better than this competitor. And I watched the video and it was interesting, he made a very compelling case, I'd never heard of Nick Milo beforehand, but we're interested in, we have a similar interest here. And then I noticed that I was not familiar with Obsidian, but I noticed that Nick has a multi-part series on how did eight things need to know about Obsidian or something like that. So I went and watched that series. And while watching that series, in YouTube, there's a link to Nick's site, which is a course that he's created called, Linking Your Thinking. So immediately I went over and I would have purchased the site, or I would have purchased the course if I could have at that point. But Nick at the time, and I think this is still true, does open, close enrollments. So there's a waiting list signup, which I signed up for. But if you think about that journey, yes, I'm interested in Nick, but I'm interested in Nick's course. And the RBS that I'm raising my hand for is, yes, I want to know Nick's backstory, maybe the story that he tells, and it's certainly the story he tells in many of his videos, the story he tells is his journey from using tools like Evernote to collect information and being a term that he's using, an information collector to being more of a thinker and using these tools more for thinking than just collecting. So yes, he's front and center in his relationship building emails, but what got me there was really the course. Yes, I'm interested in Nick, seems like a great guy. We've had some communication with him, but I'm really interested in the course that Nick has. I'm interested in linking your thinking. So what the RBS would be in that scenario would recognize that that's really why I'm coming in. Now, if I don't believe this is true yet, but if at some point in the future, Nick has four courses and each of those courses fits a different niche, and there are others in the same space that do this. I'm thinking of another person who has a much more of an academic focus, and he has two courses at the time of this recording. One of them is general personal knowledge management. Here's how he uses a set of tools, and the other is how he uses them specifically as an academic. Well, I might be interested in one of those and not the other. So a relationship building series that was general about him wouldn't really work in that scenario because I'm more interested in the course. And one of those, not the other. So you can see where that, this can be sort of layered, very nuanced, this idea of lots of different ways. So just the question to ask yourself when you're thinking about where do you put the stake in the ground and create your first asset through the art of email, we highly suggest that the first asset that you create is a relationship building series, but context matters and asking yourself, how are people finding me? It was Andre said it could be an affiliate, sends a ton of traffic your way. Well, what's the context that the affiliate is sending that traffic? How do they describe you? How are they describing the value proposition at the other end of that click? That's the relationship building. So that's the context for the relationship building series that follows. And if that person is saying, hey, you really need to know this person, he or she is a genius in this particular area, then the relationship building series is about you and your genius in that particular area. Like that makes sense. But if the affiliate sends you a ton of traffic and that traffic is like, hey, you need to know so and so because so and so has created this course and this course is the definitive guide to XYZ, well, the relationship building series that you are offering at that point really is within the context of the definitive guide to XYZ and you're a secondary actor in that play. So anything else to add on this topic before we jump to the next one, Andre? This was, I think this is a real, this is a really nuanced, a really rich nuanced topic. Yeah, I think there's one extra use case that I'll unpack, which I think is people that have affiliates or participate in JV things, this could be relevant. So in the past, Sean and I have a mutual friend, Todd Brown and Todd's promoted the previous version of auto-VML, which is Oris Wonder Madness in the past. And because I wanted to be respectful of the people that Todd was sending my way, I created a landing page specifically for Todd. So his affiliate link went directly to that page. In fact, he had a direct link that went to that page and then the affiliate component happened on the page. So nobody else in the world saw that landing page other than the people that Todd sent over to me. So when they opted in, they could purchase on the page directly, but if they opted in, that RBS series was very specific that I know Todd and our friends, and because I know that those people, they have a stronger relationship with Todd. Todd's the one that they have the relationship with and he's transferring some of that trust over to me. I acknowledge that in that little RBS, but it was very specific about that little promotion. So that RBS was talking about Todd all the time and my relationship to Todd and there's some pictures inside there about us hanging out together. And that series that played out, that little story of heart promotion that was attached to that RBS was all about my relationship with Todd and the course that he's endorsing. And that's how you can make these these promotions extremely successful, more so than just an affiliate link that goes to a more general product page and then you just kind of hope for the best. So I think that's just an extra piece you can do and the auto email completely supports that. It's the same thing. You just recognize that context matters as Sean said and you get to rig the system in a way. And it was no, even though there was extra work in me creating a custom RBS and a story-powered promotion, well, that story-powered promotion, I could tweak and use elsewhere. And because I know that Todd was gonna send a lot of really good, high quality customers my way, that all made sense and it worked great. So I think that's another use case. Yeah, and that's a great example too of, where do we allocate our attention? Somebody's listening to this and I'm like, oh my God, there's no way. I've got 10 affiliates. It's never gonna work, right? Or whatever. And it's easy to get overwhelmed. But just think about the nuance of what you just described. Like you could have done that differently. And you could have said, like, okay, I have, let's just say that we're, let's just imagine that you and I and Todd have a conversation. We want to replicate the same thing that you just used as an example, but we want to do that for the art of email. And we have a story powered promotion about the art of email specifically, with no mention of Todd or no mention of anybody else. We could have a, we could add an email as a frame for the, an existing series at the front end of that. So that somebody who we know is coming from Todd's world, the first email that they could get, we could recognize, hey, we appreciate, we know you're coming from Todd's world. And we love Todd and here's why. And here's, we tell a couple of stories about him. And then we use that to frame up. Say, we have this seven part series about the art of email that was going to be coming up next. And we could draw their attention to two or three parts that map to Todd's seminal course, which is called E5. And we can map those two things in a single email, tell them what to look for, but then tell them to send them the RBS with a soft bridge to the story, power promotion, where we could do all of that is something that everybody gets, but we're just adding a little extra value with that framing in the beginning. And this is true for, you know, I'm thinking of people who have, let's say three different sources of traffic. You know, maybe we have someone, someone's listening to this now thinking, you know, how am I going to create three different relationship building series for all three of my sources of traffic? You say, well, maybe not. Maybe you have a core relationship building series that has the things that need to be said about the course or about you, about your worldview, whatever it is, but that each source of traffic, you put the context in the beginning in the end or the beginning, whatever it is, but you're not having to recreate a new RBS for every single thing that you do. You're framing each one so that the context matters and then transitioning smoothly. So there's so, so much to do with that. Okay, let's move on to the next question. This is fun. I forget how much fun. I just love thinking out loud about this stuff. And that's really the purpose of the workshop. I mean, we're always listening to this now. Like this is, you know, the first word in workshop is work, right? This is us working on these things. There's no, the art of email is an inch wide and a mile deep. Right? What we're trying to do here is we're trying to show you how we think about it so that you can dig your own mile deep version of it. That's really the value that we hope is communicated in the workshop. Okay, second question here. This is less question, more observation, which is I sort of think about there are lots of factors that influence somebody's journey through the art of email. And I'll just throw out the ones that I came up with and then you and I can bounce these back and forth. How do we think about this? So here, here, at least a short list of factors that we think influence how you proceed through the art of email. The first one is, do you have an existing product or service to sell? Yes or no? Because if you don't, that changes things dramatically. Second question, if you do have a product or service to sell, do you have multiple products or services to sell? Because that changes the entry point in the journey through the art of email as well. And after we go through the list, we can kind of come back at it and induce different scenarios. Another option is you may be co-creating a product with an audience, like some sort of interactive offer where you're building the audience first. And then once you've built the audience to a certain size, you're going to engage with the audience to see what is the best fit for them. And then you're going to create the course that meets your audience's needs. That's why it's interactive. And that particular strategy requires a critical mass of people at a point in time. Let's say you wanna get 10 people to sign up for your first version, well, you may need to have 100 people on an email list or 150, whatever it is. So that creates its own unique set of characteristics. Certainly open enrollment versus cohort-based enrollments. I mean, that's an enormous factor in deciding your journey through the art of email. Whether or not you have an existing list or an audience or you're building one from scratch. And certainly the last category here is, are you using the art of email creating something for yourself? Or are you using the art of email to work with clients to build systems that are inspired by the art of email? Because that leads to a whole series of questions. What do clients need to internalize and believe in order to embrace this? And then you've got this weird hybrid where you're using the art of email to get clients to you wouldn't use the art of email for. So it's sort of infinitely recursive all the way down. So do you wanna grab any of these and run with them? You wanna add anything to the list? I'm sure I didn't get everything. So I guess I'm gonna go for the infinite number of factors I didn't think about. Yeah, I guess, let me think out loud for a minute. So my journey getting from when I started to now started where I built my audience before I had anything to sell. So I was just sharing stuff on various iterations of my personal blog, on the chaffron.com initially. I used to live on there. And then in 2009, when I released my very first product, my very first entering marketing product which was Orders One to Madness. And then I got OrdersOneToMadness.com and then that was my home base. That's essentially where I lived from. So in that context, suddenly me as the person was slightly less emphasized because people were landing on OrdersOneToMadness.com because they wanted to learn about that product. So my entry points over there were always focused on the email course. And then I was attached to the email course and that's how people got to know me and that relationship went from there. So my journey was audience first and then the product came later. But at that point, it flipped over where the product then had a more of a central role and I was just the person that created it. Yeah, I think that's what I would add on that piece. Yeah, now you've sort of come back to the other end of the spectrum where it's got four courses and it gets complex. So we can go through this in just a little bit. If you have an existing product or service to sell, then the big questions that answer what are people coming for? If you have a product or a service, are they coming for you specifically or is that the context or are they coming for the product? Because if it's the product, then the RBS relationship building series you write, we talked about this in an earlier, that's more about the product. That's fine or it's a service. If it's, I ran an agency for 22 years when people would get my name from somebody but they weren't there because they wanted to learn about me and they were there because they wanted to learn about the service that I offer. So the relationship building series there would be a little bit about me and my journey and how I got there to answer some of the questions that I imagined they have, but it was really about the value that I could create for them from that experience and that expertise. Now, when you go from one product to multiple products, that it's kind of the same thing. So we could use us as an example. We have four core courses. Somebody, each one of those could have a relationship building series as an entry point. If someone's not already on our list and they come in and they think, I really interested in the art of email, then that relationship building series would be different than somebody who comes in and says, I'm just interested in sort of what you guys are up to in general versus I'm interested in sphere of influence versus durable business versus the traffic engine. So choosing where you start with that would be probably, which course, which product, where do you wanna start, start there and then just build out all of them. And that would be an easy way to think about that. Co-creating a product with an audience really is about building the audience and really building a strong relationship with them. And being upfront saying, here's who I am, here's what I'm thinking about doing. I've had this experience, I've got this burning desire to create this thing with people who are like this, whatever it is, you're having that conversation, but it's leading to a point in time where you're going to have effectively a PLS. So that's like long relationship building series slash soap opera series, however you wanna imagine that. However long it takes to build the audience that you need to build to make that initial offer. So let's just say we want to sell 10 seats and we think we need 250 people on our list to do that. Well, we need, it's not like the day we get to 250 with person we pull the trigger on a product launch series because we haven't built a relationship with that 250th person. So we have to think about the time involved in building that relationship, building up to the offer, making the offer, that's a very different set of requirements. And then certainly open enrollment versus cohort based. If it's open enrollment, it's infinitely easier, I think. Co-hort based is challenging because what about, what's let's say the cohort just closed yesterday and I'm a subscriber today and it's going to open in 90 days from now. What is my experience relative to the person who signs up on day 89? That's that there are lots of questions to answer in there. So it's, those are very different systems. And then the last one we'll mention here before I hand it back to you, Andrey, is this working with clients to, if you purchase the art of email because you want to bring more of this to the world and you're the way you resell email marketing is with the same principles that we talk about in the art of email, then really you need to figure out how to get your clients to internalize and believe these ideas, which takes some significant relationship building. But you're going to have to do some work to make good clients, probably before you accept them as clients because that's a different dynamic. So anything, I feel like I'm wondering all over the place here, anything else to add to that? Andrey, that's a, this is an intense one. We've mentioned in the past in some of the other videos that, where do you start? So, where's the most leverage? And therefore that's where you're going to focus your attention because, as you guys are beginning to see the angles, the art of email is deep, right? So for us, we don't have product-centric doorways in. So as Sean mentioned, we have our relationship building series as our emerging marketing series. If you go to slash subscribe, that's that, which is general. So for us, that was an easier thing to do. We could get the most leverage out of creating that one thing, which is a week long. So we spent a few hours writing that and then it's done. And now every person coming into our world is going to go through that. There's going to be some relationships going to start. People are going to be introduced to you, on Ren Sean and our view on the world and our courses associated with that. But at some point, we're going to have the resources and time, well, the time resources to create entry points for all of our core courses and have our more general one. So, yeah, that's just what I wanted to draw attention to. It's impossible to do everything at once. So we, you know, you kind of have to figure out where's the most leverage and then loop back later on and then start to add other pieces. So it's always going to be harder if you've got multiple courses and stuff. If you're only going to go one thing, then it's always going to be easier. And, you know, then you just do stuff, tick the boxes, put it out there and start to go deeper step by step. But for us, where our front end is quite wide, we've started on what we feel offers us the most leverage and then we'll just loop back at some point and start to build out all those entry points that are more product focused, that then we'll then loop or join together on the value newsletter. That's where things will kind of merge together. And this reminds me of a question we got on our first Q and A that I want to, it's something we should think about, which is, you know, there are plenty of people who purchased the art of email and they have an existing list, an audience of some size, substantial amount, you know, whatever, it doesn't matter. But if you've been communicating with your list, your audience a certain way, and now you think, okay, the principles of the art of email resonate with me and that's what I want to do. So how do you make that transition? Right? And there is no definitive answer to this question. I mean, you certainly could imagine that you would have everyone on your list, you would create a soap opera series, which is effectively a relationship-building series, but that you're going to bring everybody onto the same page, essentially. So it's just to say that just for the sake of our, you have a thousand people on an email list, they've been with you anywhere between a couple of days to a year. And now you think, oh, the art of email, it's exactly how I want to start doing things, but I'm going to create a relationship-building series for everybody who enters my world. And, you know, let's say it takes you weeks or a week later, you have a multi-part relationship-building series, but only for the people who are entering your world. Well, what you could do on that same day that that becomes available for everybody who's new, that could be a soap opera series that everybody in your audience goes through. You could set it up beforehand, say, hey, I've really been thinking about lots of things I'd like to share with everybody. And rather than do that piecemeal over the next few months, I've put together seven emails, you'll get them over seven days or five emails, whatever it is, I've put together five emails where just it's my way of sharing of how I'm thinking about whatever the topic is that you relate to your audience. And it's five emails over five days about how I'm thinking about XYZ. So now you know everybody on your list has been exposed to what was effectively your new relationship-building series. You've framed it for them. And then everybody else who enters your world goes through the relationship-building series. So now everyone's on the same, essentially on the same page. Like there are just lots of ways you can think about about doing this. All right, let's on. This is a fun question. This next one is it's easily overlooked. And I think it's critically important that we share how we think about it. So the question is, what about people? How does the art of email relate? How does it fit in for people who want to buy immediately? And I'll kind of go through a couple of points and then like same thing we've just been doing. And I hand it back to Andre and we're just gonna riff on this a little bit. And this is pretty nuanced. So just to set the stage, some percentage of people, I'm one of these people, I tend to decide I'm going to buy pretty quickly. And I'll use the example I used earlier with Nick Milo. If Nick's course had been available, I would have gone from YouTube video to landing page to purchase in one shot. Like I felt like Nick was credible. Like the YouTube video sold me essentially. And I was just looking for a buy button. But because Nick doesn't have open enrollment, I had to add myself to a list and I could buy later. So if you have an evergreen offer, and we at the time of this recording, we have four evergreen courses, there are some percentage of people, and we see this almost every day, there are some people who come into our world, they find the course, that's what they're interested in, and they buy it right then. Well, we still want to build a relationship with them. It's not like we said, oh, good, now that you're a customer, we don't have to worry about all that fuzzy stuff, the hugs and kisses, we just move on. It's not that at all. So this is where the customer onboarding series fits in, that that has two things that it needs to do really well. One, it needs to help the person who's purchase get value from that purchase. However, that's defined by you and your offers. And make sure that we're building a solid relationship around the context of that purchase, but that we're taking the time to really emphasize that relationship. That could be functional things, like how to get in touch with support, if they have questions, how to log into their portal, all of those things, it could also be drawing their attention over time to the ways that they can get the most value from what they purchased, how they can interact with all of those things, but we don't want to forget about the relationship because we got the sale. That's not at all how it works. The relationship building component of the art of email exists independent of the sale. So it's not like once we finally get somebody to buy, we have to stop all that nonsense. It's not it at all. A couple of other points to make about this before I hand it off to Andre is, recognize that those initial sales, they're part of a much larger signal. I mean, this is the whole idea that Dean Jackson talked about, that a minority of sales happened early, the majority of people were going to buy by later. So when you see that there are sales happening, don't make the mistake of then starting to optimize around those initial sales because they're just part of a much larger signal. So, and we don't want to optimize for that signal at the expense of the larger number of sales that waits on the other side of a building relationship. So let's just say for the sake of argument that every day a hundred people enter your world in two of them buy. I should have done better math here so I can do the math easier. But like the two who buy don't then, what's so common for people to do is to be like, oh, how could I make that two five? And then, you know, they crank up the pressure and they do all these things, but those two are just a signal of what's likely 10. That's actually the math. Okay, I did it all right. So it's those two are actually a signal of what's potentially over, you know, beyond 90 days, 10 plus. So don't make decisions at that initial part that are going to compromise the 10 later. Recognize that that's just the nature of some people who buy they buy early and fast, but continue to focus on building the relationship both with those people and with everybody else so that the real opportunity isn't missed. And then the last point I'll make on this one is to match the, match emails to the business model. So that, you know, for example, a purchaser gets a customer onboarding series, a lead who doesn't purchase would get a relationship building series. And if perhaps you retarget visitors of certain qualities, say somebody who sees your landing page for a certain period of time or a certain depth but doesn't buy that you might retarget them with an opt-in, but that leads to a relationship building series, right? That this, we're taking a step back, we're asking ourselves all the time, okay, we're matching what we do, what the art of email does systemically to the behavior of the person. So the person who comes to our site or landing page and buys they see a customer onboarding series, someone who doesn't buy, but shows a significant level of interest, right then gets relationship building series. And then maybe people who are as a sufficient quality who choose to retarget, the opt-in is about the relationship building series. It's not about trying to get them to purchase again, they've said no to the purchase. So let's respect that, but they've also shown some interest and let's show them the next thing being potential for building that relationship with them. So back to the original question, Andre, what about people who want to buy immediately riff on your thoughts about that and how it fits into the art of email? Yeah, so let me unpack that as it relates to the customer onboarding series and then also look back to a previous question you asked, well, you mentioned about somebody who already has a functioning business and now wants to implement art of email and they've just written a brand new RBS and then what do they do with their current audience? So as we're recording this, art of email is we busy dispensing that to our customers and there is no, this is gonna sound terrible, but there is no customer onboarding series for art of email right now. It's a single email that's just acknowledged as somebody's made a purchase and they then get access to the course, which is not how we normally do things, but in this context, that's how it exists because we wanted to wait until we've delivered the entire course and then any nuances and any customer feedback that we're getting over the course of this month, we wanna incorporate that into customer onboarding series for art of email, of which we've written that we're gonna be tweaking it, but it's not live yet. So once we've written that, which is after we've delivered this course and this workshop, we're gonna put that live inside of our emails service. And at that point, we will then send, essentially it's a hard bridge. It'll go to all of our customers, including all of our long, long, customers from all respond to madness that some of them have come in over a decade ago. So we're not automatically gonna trigger that brand new customer onboarding series to every single customer we have now because it's gonna be jarring for some of them that have had access to all respond to madness for years and years and years and even a decade plus. And suddenly they receive this onboarding series out of the blue. So we will send out the hard bridge saying that we've created the brand new art of email, customer onboarding series. If you want to receive it, click here, which then just adds them to the series and they will receive it as if they've just made a purchase and purchased auto email. Every other person that's purchasing auto email will get that series in that moment live as it's intended, which is not the case right now. So that's the first thing I wanted to link to that previous observation you made. And then jump in for one second too. Now, let me just jump in on this because I think there's something really cool to point out that will be fun. So two things, one, I just wanna mention that we do, we are communicating with broadcast emails with this group until we do what you just described. So I wanna make sure everyone realizes where it's not like we, everyone didn't just buy and we're like, yep, we're dark until the course is done. So we are communicating, but it's manual and every day of every Monday when we publish content, we manually send out an email that says, hey, this is the content that's there. But the other thing that's really fascinating, this is where I love the richness and layers of the art of email is that we could make the decision to send out an email that says, let's just say someone buys the art of email after this whole customer onboarding series is ready to go and somebody buys it. And we could say, hey, welcome to the art of email. We have a whatever, a 30 day onboarding series, but you get to choose how you receive that. So, and we can have sort of three options. Option number one, hey, we appreciate it guys, but I'm just not, I'm just gonna look at the course. I don't need the emails. Great, then we could put in parentheses, you don't have to do anything, whatever. And then option two, daily emails over the next 30 days, and we could describe a little value proposition where we unpack all of the nuances from each module and order, great. Or then after that, we could say, weekly summary, which in parentheses, just tell me two or three things each week, how to get the most value from the art of email. So again, it's just the Lego idea of all these building blocks that when you have this capability to do these different bridge methods, the bridge doesn't have to be binary where you get it or you don't. The bridge also can be, I get this thing, but I want the thing this way. Now, of course you have to be willing to create that. But if you think about it, if you create, say you create 30 days of onboarding emails, within those 30 days, there are probably, you could probably make a four part high level series from that content for weekly over four weeks. So instantly you have this extra layer of nuance. So that what you just said inspired that. So now I'm gonna hopefully not have screwed up your train of thought too much to get you back to the what about people who don't buy immediately question. Yeah, okay. So somebody's just made a purchase and they now, and they, and they're not in our world. So they've just come to our landing page, they've, they see the value there or someone's referred them. So they really know that they wanna buy this thing and they make a purchase. So the very first email that they get from us is the customer onboarding series. Now, at this point, we know the value that's inside of the customer onboarding series for whichever product that they've purchased. And how I've done this in the past is, and this works so incredibly well that a customer onboarding series can be, you know, I'm just gonna throw out a subjective number here, but it can be 10 times more potent or more powerful than a normal relationship building series where somebody's coming into a world that's either general or product specific, but it's different. They're not a customer at that point. So you're limited to how you can communicate and interact with them. But when someone's raised their hand and they put money down and they've made a purchase, that onboarding series can be very, very different. Like the level of relationship that can be established through that is next level in the sense that the way that I've done this historically is somebody buys the product. So they get access to the product that they purchased. But I've always rigged my customer onboarding series to be an extension of the product, but delivered in a way that talks about things that is not in the product. So it's extra stuff like nuances, different ways of thinking about something. Kind of like we're doing right now in this conversation that we're having now, there's stuff that we're gonna be talking about that's not inside of the course, inside of the main course. So I've always made my customer onboarding series, whether they three or four weeks long, but it takes people on a journey. I know that they have access to the course. So in the arc of that customer onboarding series, it's maybe breaking the course up into little pieces and then maybe three emails talk about each piece. So it just impacts different angles of nuances about a certain module, for example, all done in this narrative form that they can point people back to the course. So don't forget, module three, and it points back to module three, but that email impacts it in a way that's very different. And in some cases, like with a sphere of influence, customer onboarding series, there's stuff inside there that's not in the course because the email sequence is part of the course. It's an extension of the course delivered through email. And it's more powerful because it's forcing people to go through it piece by piece in an order that I know that it's gonna produce a certain impact with them. So by the time a brand new customer goes through, through the entirety of a customer onboarding series, that relationship is pretty deep, more so than just a normal RBS. So that's what I wanted to add with that. Yeah, and that's funny, I'm laughing because we jumped way ahead to customer onboarding series and we said we were going to take this week by week, but it's just such a beautiful example of it. It's a module that exists in both worlds. In the trust engine and the revenue engine, but in ways that tie them together. That's just a nice example to think about early for a use case that's very common. And it points the way to the richness of the art of email and how nuanced it is. But these are not hard categories. Like this is just relationship. This is just revenue. It's never like that. They all exist together and tugging on one little part of it, it influences all the other parts of it. That's a principle of systems. There is that every part is connected to every other part. We can't just change one part without affecting the whole. So the customer onboarding series exists in both of these worlds in a way that it's, Andre just described, it's just heightened more intense relationship because someone has taken a big step toward us from the perspective of trust and said, here's my money. And the relationship we build in that context is perfect. It's much more intense because of that trust that's given to us and the potential concern about was that trust well placed. Like that context exists. It's the reality of the post purchase situation for that prospect, that person who's now a customer, a paying customer. And we're just recognizing that for the customer onboarding series in a way that's different than saying someone's just raised their hand and taken a step towards us to opt in that intensity is very different and allows us to meet that person's needs very specifically. All right, two more questions. Let me just add one extra thing. I know we're gonna have an Italian module on the customer onboarding series, but just wanna say one extra thing, which is the fact that, and again, I don't have like hard data to back up what I'm about to say because we've only ever done it one way, but one of the reasons why we think that our refund rates are so ridiculously low is because of our customer onboarding series. Somebody receives that and starts to receive that series. They just don't wanna refund because yes, they have access to the entirety of a course that they've just purchased. But the piece that gets dripped out bit by bit is this relationship that starts to blossom over the course of multiple weeks. And by the time they get to the end of that, it's like the last thing in their mind is to refund. It's just not gonna happen. So yeah, that's just one extra thing to think about. That's a great point. All right, next question. This came up in a Q&A and it's one of those things I've been thinking about since. So I'll draw a little attention to it. So one of the questions that was asked is, what we say we're writers, not copywriters and the implication was, is that a pejorative comment about copywriting? Like, are we saying, when we said that, did we mean like we don't like copywriting? And that's not what we were saying at all. In the context that we said, we're writers, not copywriters, we were trying to say, you don't have to be a copywriter to do what we were talking about. We're not, we're writers. And we write conversationally, but there's a broader answer to this too that I think is important for everyone to understand because many of you listening to this either have some experience in copywriting or thinking about getting some experience in copywriting. And we're not saying there's anything wrong with that. We wanna be very clear about that. I think the thing to say now is some of our best friends are copywriters. That's true, like we get it. But the way to think about this is that copywriting is a method of learning to write that gets people to buy. That's what copywriting is all about. The intent of copywriting is to get people to buy. Most of the content that we create in the systems that we build with the art of email is focused on building and enhancing relationships and deepening trust over time, not selling. So it's this becomes a, what's the best tool for the job? Well, the primary job that we have over time is to capture the attention of an audience and maintain that audience's attention over long periods of time, sometimes focusing it around certain topics, other times just maintaining it over long periods of time. Well, what's the best tool for that? Well, from a writing perspective, to capture and hold attention, the, we believe the best method for that is embodies and principles of serialized narratives. Serialized narratives capture an audience's attention and they hold that attention and they pull that attention forward toward a known destination or an unknown destination. They pull it forward. So if that's, if our primary job is to capture it and maintain and pull forward an audience's attention and the best way to do that is with serialized narrative, then that to us just seems like that's kind of a logical approach to take. It doesn't mean don't learn copywriting, but what I do think it means is if copywriting is the only lens that you have on the world and that lens is emphasized selling, most of what you write is going to sound like you're selling, even if you're not actually selling something. And this is from my observation, I've worked with some, some large direct response companies that try to do relationship building emails, but those are almost always written by copywriters. And even though they're not deliberately trying to sell in relationship building emails, they still sound like they're selling everything because that's, and why wouldn't they? They're copywriters, that's what they're doing. So this is a broad statement, but it's, we want to be clear about this idea that yes, if you want to learn copywriting, that's fine. Just make sure that you're not, that you're not thinking about writing relationship building emails like a copywriter. Like the better method is found in module two, the principles of serialized narrative. That method for maintaining an audience attention over time, capturing and maintaining an audience of attention over time is the better tool for the job that is most of the work that we do. And yes, you can then switch gears and use copywriting when you sell, but that to me feels, sure use the principles of copywriting when it's time to sell. But if you've built a world around relationships and narrative and pulling attention forward with that structure, it just seems like the logical conclusion to keep doing that, not have a disconnect when you quote unquote are selling. They just maintain that same vibe. And Andre, do you have thoughts on this from a same different perspective? Yeah, I think that's, you've said it better than I could have said it's, yeah, we're not poking sticks at copywriters, even though as Sean said, many of our friends are copywriters and it's just for us, when we have a system that emphasizes relationships, we do, the selling for the most part happens almost automatically. Yes, we have specific, the components of the revenue engine are very specific and we do emphasize selling at that point, but it's built on the back of us having established relationships first. So, I mean, you can go through the rest of your life never opening or looking at a copywriting course and you'll be just fine. And if you are a copywriter, I think learning the principles of serialized narrative will only enhance what you do. And it's really, it comes down to this idea too, of like, do sales happen deliberately? Like we're doing something and we're making the sales happen and it's sort of very cause and effect, Newtonian view of the world, or are sales an emergent property of a larger system? And from our perspective and the world that we see that we believe reflects reality of our experience, in general, it seems like a very small percentage of sales that happen are deliberate and causal and that most are emergent from other things that have happened. Certainly our own buying behavior is like that. So, that just seems like a better fit that if you create a system where the system, the way the parts interact produces this emergent property of selling, that just feels, whatever reason to us feels better, more right, more accurate than trying to deliberately engineer cause and effect relationship all the time. That just doesn't feel like that's how the world actually works. Okay, this is the last, this is actually a good segue to the last question. And this is funny, this may be one where we completely disagree or we completely agree. Fascinating to see where this goes. So I think I'm gonna trick you on this one. Not tricky, but I'm gonna put you in the hot seat. So I'm going to ask the question of you first and then I'm gonna give my answer. So here's the question. Funnels in world building, can they coexist? So let's assume you're listening to this and you have, and we're not throwing stones at anybody to be very clear about this, that you have a ClickFunnels account, a single funnel, a paid ad on Facebook, and your entire business is sending traffic from a Facebook ad to a ClickFunnels funnel and you're getting some, and it doesn't matter what the offer is, none of that matters, but that's your entire business. And then you purchase the art of email and think, okay, world building, that's not like funnels. So do I need to get rid of my ClickFunnels account and start all over in order to embrace world building? So can they coexist? Do you think in your world, Andre, in your view of the world? Yes, I'm gonna say that my lens is just, I don't name them like that. I don't think of them like that. And although, semantics kind of matter sometimes and it could be a quote unquote funnel funnel, like a ClickFunnels funnel, I think it's easy to adapt that, almost detune it to a sense where instead of hiding all the links that actually go to the world, like many ClickFunnels or funnel funnels, like you go into a typical direct response marketing funnel and there's no links anywhere. I mean, like there's no navigation, it's all disappeared. In fact, even the videos don't even have back buttons or stop buttons if you really wanna go crazy with the stuff. And that's just, that's not how we see the world. And I think I'm gonna, I'm gonna show it in with this, with what I've just said, but yeah, funnels exist and they obviously work well, but I think if you really wanna create a art of email inspired system over time, I think some of those funnels can be de-emphasized in the sense that show navigation, allow some people, certainly put navigation on your videos and allow people to be able to stop a video if they're having lunch or doing something else and have navigation that kind of spills out into your blog where they can find articles and interact with your content and see other stuff. And don't think that these people are gonna be lost forever because maybe some of them will, but maybe some of them will bond better because they've now been exposed. I know many of these it sounds like I'm picking on click funnels whenever I talk about funnels, but you're the one that mentioned click funnels, but I think many of these click funnels, many of these funnel type businesses, there is no world building. I mean, in the sense that they're probably even show navigation even if they wanted to because there's nothing to point people to. It is just linear ABC, you know, give me your money or get onto my email list thing. And I think that's not how we see it. And I think, you know, our view on the world is world building is central to that. And it creates effects that just can't be measured all the time. It's just, I can't tell you how many emails that are short on our get that people have said that the Landerana website, they've never heard of us before and they've landed on a website and they've just read five, like all of our series emails, they've just, but not on our email list, on our website, the ones that are on the website, they've read all these things. And when we go back and count, they could have read literally 10,000 words or tens of thousands of words of content to the point where they've now gone to our contact page and they've been inspired to reach out to us because they're like, well, this is completely shattered because they've just experienced this moment that was unique to them because they read things that they wanted to read. Any other person lands on our website isn't gonna have that same experience and they won't read the same place or things because that person just followed a thread. They landed on something, they read it, they put all the thread, they went to another page, they read that and it just kind of meandered around for a bit to the point where they had to reach out. And those people that reach out to us in that context, even when they haven't purchased anything yet, Sean and I just like smile to each other because those people, that person's gonna become a customer, just wait and see. And we can check days later, a week later, a month later, and they're a customer. And in many cases, they've just suddenly purchased, if not all the products, but multiple courses because of that experience. And when you remove world-building out of the picture, that can never, ever happen. The only people that you can ever interact with are the ones that are willing to go A, B, C, watch videos that don't have any navigation and just hope for the best. That's my rant over. Yeah, it's funny. I thought we were gonna disagree, but that's comical that I thought that. So you've given the answer, so I don't really need to go any further with that. I think the way I would approach is very similar. Yes, I mean, if you have a business, that's a funnel-centric business where you're sending traffic to an offer and you're getting some percentage of people who become customers and you want to do more, there's nothing, we're not casting stones at that business model at all. We just know that the data is very clear, other people's experience is very clear, that the response to that particular business model is a very small fraction of what is actually available from that same audience. So for every hundred people who go in that business model, even a highly optimized one, that the signal that you're getting from that model is a small fraction of what's actually available from the traffic you're already paying for. And what we're simply saying is that when you layer on, when you use the framework of world building and you begin to expand that one layer at a time that you don't have to do it, it's not an all or none thing, we're not saying get rid of your funnels, but when you begin to expand out with additional content, additional touch points, additional ways for people to meet their needs as they relate to the value you create, then the rest of that audience you're already paying for, if it's paid traffic becomes accessible to you just over a longer time horizon. Those people, they're already there, they're already waiting, and all they're asking is that we don't screw it up. And world building is a really good framework to recognize that that signal we got early is only part of the signal, and that the much larger signal is waiting for us when we give all these other touch points and other interactions through email and the building of worlds in different ways and different contexts, it's all accessible there from what we've already invested in. So yes, funnels, world building absolutely can coexist. Funnels are just part of the larger world, that's all. All right, I think we've done it for the day, wow. That was longer than I thought. Well, not bombshell. Yeah, really, on that one, we'll call it a day. All right, thanks everybody for your attention, as always, and get back to work.
